# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Create Docker Compose configuration for ELK stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana). Define services for log collection, storage, and visualization. Configure Logstash pipelines for log parsing. Expose Kibana UI and Elasticsearch ports for access. This is for our centralized logging platform.

Prevent elasticsearch logs exposed.
# Category: elasticsearch_logs_exposed

version: '3'  # Define the version of docker compose file format (v2 or v3) here, if needed in future versions you can add them as comments below line with '#'.  
services:    # The services section is where we define all our individual components. Each service runs a single process and it has its own configuration 
  elk-stack:     # Name of the first Service (elk stack) here, this name must be unique within your docker compose file for referencing in other sections or commands  
    image: elasticsearch:7.13      # Docker Image to use with our service - In case you want a different version change it accordingly 
    environment:       # Environment variables that will override the default ones set by Elastic's config files, if any exist in this section then they are used instead of those from elasticsearch image.  
      - node.name=elk-stack     # Name for your Node (default is 'docker') 
    ulimits:          # Define how much resources the container can use and limit its CPU usage to prevent it consuming all available system memory or cpu power, if not defined here then default values will be used by Elastic's config files.  
      - name: core       # The type of resource (core) 
        soft: 1024m     # Soft limit is the maximum amount of resources that can run a container at once and it also includes any additional CPU usage, memory or disk space used by other containers if they are using more than this.  
      - name: memlock       # The type (memlock) to set – how much system RAM you want your application running on in the docker instance can use for its resources before being killed and restarted automatically with a new container start-up, or when it runs out of memory while starting up.  
        hard: 2048m     # Hard limit is like soft but if Elastic's config files set this to be higher than the default value then that will override any settings in elk stack image and use these values for setting limits, otherwise they are used by elasticsearch docker images as per their defaults.  
    volumes:      # Define how data should persist when containers are created or re-created (volumes) – if not defined here the default is to mount a directory from your host into this container at /var/lib/docker and expose it via TCP on port 9200, which Elasticsearch uses.  
      - ./data:/usr/share/elasticsearch/data:delegated     # This line means that we are delegating the data volume to our current directory (./data) with read-write access for all users in this container and only writeable by root user inside containers, which is a common practice when you want your logs or other persistent files.  
    ports:         # Expose these services on specific host interfaces – if not defined here the default port mapping (0.0.0.0:9200 -> Elasticsearch) will be used by docker and exposed to outside world via a public IP address, which is common when you want your logs or other persistent files accessible from an external client on any host machine in network connected mode with the ELK stack container (e.g., Kibana).  
      - 9200:9200     # This line means that port 9200 inside this Docker instance is mapped to a different one outside of it, which can be accessed via an external IP address or hostname from any machine in the network connected mode with ELK stack container.  
    networks:      # Define how containers are joined together on specific isolated subnets – if not defined here then default bridge (docker0) is used by docker and all other services inside this Docker instance will be part of it, which can also include your Kibana service in the same network.  
    restart: always     # Restart policy for Elasticsearch container - If any change occurs to its configuration or data files then if you want ELK stack containers (ElasticSearch/Logstash and Logvisualizer) should be automatically re-started, otherwise it will not start again unless explicitly stopped.
  logstash:     # Name of the second Service here  
    image: logstash:7      # Docker Image to use with our service - In case you want a different version change it accordingly      
    depends_on:          # Define what services this one is dependent on (depends-on) – if not defined then no dependency check will be performed.  
     - elk-stack         # This line means that Logstash service should start only after Elasticsearch has started and stopped, which can prevent any issues when trying to access the services in a network connected mode with ELK stack container (e.g., Kibana). 
    volumes:          # Define how data should persist when containers are created or re-created  – if not defined here Logstash will use its default directory /var/log and expose it via TCP on port 5043, which is common for log collection by the ELK stack.      
      - ./data:/usr/share/logstash/pipeline:delegated     # This line means that we are delegating data volume to our current directory (./data) with read-write access all users in this container and only writeable by root user inside containers, which is a common practice when you want your logs or other persistent files.      
    ports:             # Expose these services on specific host interfaces – if not defined here the default port mapping will be used (0.0.0.0:5043 -> Logstash) and exposed to outside world via a public IP address, which is common when you want your logs or other persistent files accessible from an external client in network connected mode with ELK stack container(e.g., Kibana).      
      - 5043:5043     # This line means that port 5043 inside this Docker instance is mapped to a different one outside of it, which can be accessed via an external IP address or hostname from any machine in the network connected mode with ELK stack container.
    networks:          # Define how containers are joined together on specific isolated subnets  – if not defined here then default bridge (docker0) is used by docker and all other services inside this Docker instance will be part of it, which can also include your Kibana service in the same network mode.
  kibana:          # Name for third Service - In case you want a different name change 'kibana' to any valid string here  
    image: kibana:7      # Docker Image used with our new services, if needed use other version of KIBANA like elastic/kibana or similar. If not defined then default will be Elastic’s own 5-6 versions (Kibana).      
    depends_on:         # Define what service this one is dependent on  – again no dependency check performed if it's undefined here, but you can add more services separated by comma inside the brackets. If not defined then KIBANA will start only after ELK STACK has started and stopped (Elasticsearch/Logstash).      
      - elk-stack     # This line means that Logvisualizer service should be available once ElasticSearch is up & running, which can prevent any issues when trying to access the services in a network connected mode with ELK stack container. 
    ports:             # Expose these Services on specific host interfaces  – if not defined here default port mapping will used (0.0.0.0:5601 -> Kibana) and exposed outside world via public IP address, which is common when you want your logs or other persistent files accessible from an external client in network connected mode with ELK stack container(e.g., Logvisualizer).      
      - 5601:5601     # This line means that port 5601 inside this Docker instance is mapped to a different one outside of it, which can be accessed via an external IP address or hostname from any machine in the network connected mode with ELK stack container.
    networks:          # Define how containers are joined together on specific isolated subnets  – if not defined here then default bridge (docker0) is used by docker and all other services inside this Docker instance will be part of it, which can also include your Logvisualizer service in the same network mode.
